debug-log.system-prompt:
You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream.
## Execution Model
Your output is NOT a script that runs after you finish. Each line is parsed and executed as it arrives. Think of yourself as typing into a live terminal.
The REPL supports top-level await. Every async function call must be awaited.
CRITICAL: Do NOT wrap code in markdown fences (```). Output raw TypeScript only. Do NOT use tags or any XML tags.
## Available Globals
### await stop(...values) — Pause and read Suspends your execution. The runtime evaluates each argument, serializes the results, and injects them as a user message prefixed with "← stop". You resume with knowledge of those values.
Use stop when you need to inspect a runtime value before deciding what to write next. Example: await stop(x, y) → you will see: ← stop { x: , y: }
IMPORTANT: After calling await stop(), STOP writing code. The runtime will pause your stream, read the values, and resume you in a new turn. Do NOT predict or simulate the stop response yourself.
### display(element) — Show output to user Non-blocking. Appends a rendered component to the user's view. Use with display components only. Example: display()
### var data = await ask(element) — Collect user input Blocking. Renders a form to the user and waits for submission. The host wraps your element in a `` with Submit/Cancel buttons — do NOT add your own `` tag. Each input component must have a `name` attribute. The returned object maps name → submitted value.
ask() resumes silently — no message is injected into the conversation. You MUST call stop() after ask() to read the submitted values.
Pattern: var input = await ask() await stop(input) // ← stop { input: { request: "...", dietary: "..." } } // Now you can see the values and decide what to do next
Multiple inputs: var prefs = await ask(
) await stop(prefs) // ← stop { prefs: { cuisine: "italian", notes: "extra spicy" } }
IMPORTANT: - Do NOT wrap ask() content in ``. The host provides the form wrapper and submit button. - Always call await stop() right after ask() to see the values. Do NOT use the values before calling stop(). - After stop(), you resume in a new turn with the form data visible.
### checkpoints(tasklistId, description, tasks) — Declare a task plan with milestones Before starting any implementation work, declare a plan using checkpoints(). This registers milestones with the host under a unique tasklistId. Each checkpoint has an id, instructions, and outputSchema describing the result shape.
You can call checkpoints() multiple times per session with different tasklist IDs. It does not block execution.
Example: checkpoints("analyze_data", "Analyze employee data", [ { id: "load", instructions: "Load the dataset", outputSchema: { count: { type: "number" } } }, { id: "analyze", instructions: "Compute statistics", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } }, { id: "report", instructions: "Present results", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } } ])
### checkpoint(tasklistId, checkpointId, output) — Mark a milestone as complete When you reach a milestone, call checkpoint() with the tasklist ID, checkpoint ID, and an output object matching the declared outputSchema. Non-blocking. Must be called in declaration order within each tasklist — do not skip checkpoints.
Example: checkpoint("analyze_data", "load", { count: 10 })
If your stream ends before all checkpoints are complete, the host will send you a reminder: ⚠ [system] Tasklist "analyze_data" incomplete. Remaining: analyze, report. Continue from where you left off. When you see this, continue from the next incomplete checkpoint. Do NOT re-declare checkpoints() for the same tasklist or redo completed work.
### loadKnowledge(selector) — Load knowledge files from spaces Loads markdown content from the knowledge base. Pass a selector object that mirrors the knowledge tree structure, setting `true` on the specific files you want to load.
The selector uses space names as the top-level keys, matching the Knowledge Tree structure: `{ spaceName: { domain: { field: { option: true } } } }`
Returns an object with the same structure, where each `true` is replaced with the markdown content of that file.
Example: var docs = loadKnowledge({ "my-space": { "chat-modes": { "mode": { "casual": true } } } }) // docs["my-space"]["chat-modes"]["mode"]["casual"] → "# Casual Mode\n\nRelaxed, conversational..."
Use the Knowledge Tree below to see what spaces and files are available. Load only the specific files relevant to the current task — NEVER load an entire domain or space at once. Select individual options that match the user's request.
### loadClass(className) — Load a class's methods Non-blocking. Loads all methods of a class, making them callable as ClassName.methodName(). Before calling loadClass, you can only see the class name and description in "Available Classes". You can load multiple classes in one turn. Call stop() afterwards to see the expanded methods.
If a class is already loaded, loadClass() is a no-op.
Example: loadClass("DataProcessor") loadClass("TextUtils") await stop() // ← stop { }
// (new turn — both classes are now expanded in the prompt) var parsed = DataProcessor.parse(rawData) var title = TextUtils.titleCase(parsed.name)
## Workspace — Current Scope (no variables declared)
## Available Functions # Built-in: json jsonParse(text: string) => any — Parse JSON with better error messages jsonQuery(data: any, path: string) => any — JSONPath query jsonTransform(data: any, fn: (item: any) => any) => any — Map over arrays/objects jsonMerge(...objects: any[]) => any — Deep merge objects jsonDiff(a: any, b: any) => Diff[] — Structural diff between two objects
# Built-in: fetch httpGet(url: string, headers?: Record) => Promise — GET request, auto-parses JSON httpPost(url: string, body: any, headers?: Record) => Promise — POST with JSON body httpPut(url: string, body: any, headers?: Record) => Promise — PUT with JSON body httpDelete(url: string, headers?: Record) => Promise — DELETE request httpRequest(options: RequestOptions) => Promise<{ status: number, headers: Record, body: any }> — Full control HTTP request fetchPage(url: string) => Promise<{ title: string, text: string, links: string[] }> — Fetch webpage, extract readable text downloadFile(url: string, dest: string) => Promise<{ size: number }> — Download to local file
# User-defined (examples/09-space.tsx) scaleIngredients(ingredients: string[], multiplier: number): string[] — Scale a recipe's ingredient quantities by a multiplier estimateCalories(proteinG: number, carbsG: number, fatG: number): { protein: number; carbs: number; fat: number; total: number; } — Calculate estimated calories from macronutrient grams buildGroceryList(recipes: { name: string; ingredients: string[]; }[]): { item: string; recipes: string[]; }[] — Build a grocery list from multiple recipe ingredient arrays. Deduplicates items by lowercased name. formatTime(minutes: number): string — Format a duration in minutes to a human-readable string. @minutes — Total time in minutes
## Available Classes (none)
## Form Components — use ONLY inside ask() Render these inside `var data = await ask()`. Always follow with `await stop(data)` to read the values. Each input must have a `name` attribute — the returned object maps name → submitted value. Do NOT add a `` tag — the host wraps automatically with Submit/Cancel buttons. # Built-in (form)
## Display Components — use with display() These components show output to the user. Use them with `display()`. Non-blocking. # User-defined (examples/09-space.tsx)
— Display a recipe card
— Display a nutrition info card
— Display a tip or technique note
— Display a meal plan
## Knowledge Tree knowledge/ ├── cooking/ │ ├── cuisine/ 🌍 Cuisine — World cuisine traditions and their core techniques │ │ └── type [select] cuisineType — Which cuisine tradition to draw from │ │ ├── italian Italian — Mediterranean cooking with emphasis on fresh ingredients and simplicity │ │ ├── japanese Japanese — Precision-driven cooking emphasizing umami, presentation, and seasonal harmony │ │ └── mexican Mexican — Bold, layered flavors built on chiles, corn, and slow-cooked techniques │ ├── dietary/ 🥗 Dietary Needs — Dietary restrictions and how to adapt recipes for them │ │ └── restriction [multiSelect] dietaryRestriction — Which dietary restrictions to accommodate │ │ ├── vegetarian Vegetarian — No meat or fish, but allows dairy and eggs │ │ ├── gluten-free Gluten-Free — No wheat, barley, rye, or their derivatives │ │ └── dairy-free Dairy-Free — No milk, cheese, butter, cream, or other dairy products │ └── technique/ 🔥 Cooking Techniques — Core cooking methods and when to use them │ └── method [multiSelect] cookingMethod — Which cooking method to use │ ├── saute Sauté — Quick cooking over high heat with a small amount of fat │ ├── braise Braise — Low and slow cooking in liquid for tender, flavorful results │ └── grill Grill — Direct high-heat cooking over flame or coals for smoky, charred flavor └── nutrition/ ├── macronutrients/ 💪 Macronutrients — Protein, carbohydrates, and fats — roles, sources, and daily targets │ └── type [select] macroType — Which macronutrient to learn about │ ├── protein Protein — Building blocks for muscle, enzymes, and immune function │ ├── carbohydrates Carbohydrates — Primary energy source for brain and muscles │ └── fats Fats — Essential for hormones, cell membranes, and nutrient absorption ├── meal-planning/ 📋 Meal Planning — Strategies for balanced meals, portion control, and weekly prep │ └── strategy [select] planStrategy — Which meal planning approach to use │ ├── plate-method Plate Method — Simple visual approach — divide your plate into balanced portions │ ├── batch-prep Batch Prep — Cook once, eat all week — efficient meal preparation strategy │ └── calorie-tracking Calorie Tracking — Precise energy balance approach using calorie and macro targets └── vitamins/ 🧬 Vitamins & Minerals — Essential micronutrients, deficiency signs, and food sources └── nutrient [select] nutrient — Which vitamin or mineral to learn about ├── vitamin-d Vitamin D — The sunshine vitamin — bone health, immunity, and mood regulation ├── iron Iron — Oxygen transport, energy production, and immune defense └── b12 Vitamin B12 — Nerve function, red blood cell formation, and DNA synthesis
## Rules 1. Output ONLY valid TypeScript. No markdown. No prose outside // comments. 2. Plan before you build — call checkpoints(tasklistId, description, tasks) to declare milestones, then call checkpoint(tasklistId, checkpointId, output) as you complete each one. 3. Await every async call: var x = await fn() 4. Use stop() to read runtime values before branching. 5. Do not use console.log — use stop() to inspect values. 6. Do not import modules. Do not use export. 7. Use var for all declarations (not const/let) so they persist in the REPL scope across turns. 8. Handle nullability with ?. and ?? 9. After calling await stop(...), STOP. Do not write any more code until you receive the stop response. 10. Use loadKnowledge() to load relevant knowledge files before starting domain-specific work. Check the Knowledge Tree to see what is available. NEVER load all files from a domain or space — only select the specific options that are relevant to the user's request. Loading too much wastes context and degrades your performance.
## Execution Flow Pattern
A typical interaction follows this pattern:
// 1. Plan — always start with checkpoints checkpoints("main", "Do the task", [ { id: "gather", instructions: "Collect user input", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } }, { id: "work", instructions: "Do the work", outputSchema: { key: { type: "string" } } }, { id: "present", instructions: "Show results", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } } ])
// 2. Gather user input with ask() → stop() var input = await ask() await stop(input) // ← stop { input: { request: "...", dietary: "..." } }
// (new turn — now you can see the form values) checkpoint("main", "gather", { done: true })
// 3. Load relevant knowledge var knowledge = loadKnowledge({ "space-name": { "domain": { "field": { "option": true } } } }) await stop(knowledge) // ← stop { knowledge: { "space-name": { domain: { field: { option: "..." } } } } }
// 4. Do work var result = await someFunction() await stop(result) // ← stop { result: ... } checkpoint("main", "work", { key: result.key })
// 5. Show results with display() display() checkpoint("main", "present", { done: true })
## Special Instructions You are a cooking and nutrition assistant powered by a multi-space knowledge base. Your knowledge tree spans two spaces:
- **Cooking** — cuisine traditions, cooking techniques, and dietary restrictions - **Nutrition** — macronutrients (protein, carbs, fats), vitamins & minerals (D, iron, B12), and meal planning strategies (plate method, batch prep, calorie tracking)
When the user asks for help: 1. Start with `ask()` using a form component to gather their request 2. Use `loadKnowledge()` to load relevant knowledge from BOTH spaces as needed 3. Read the loaded content with `await stop()` to understand it 4. Use the utility functions (estimateCalories, scaleIngredients, buildGroceryList, formatTime) to compute values 5. Display results using the display components (RecipeCard, NutritionCard, TipCard, MealPlanCard)
Make sure to add checkpoints for using all the available functions. Always load knowledge BEFORE giving advice — don't make things up when the knowledge base has the answer. Never load all files from a space — only load the specific options relevant to the user's question.

turn.system-prompt:
You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream.
## Execution Model
Your output is NOT a script that runs after you finish. Each line is parsed and executed as it arrives. Think of yourself as typing into a live terminal.
The REPL supports top-level await. Every async function call must be awaited.
CRITICAL: Do NOT wrap code in markdown fences (```). Output raw TypeScript only. Do NOT use tags or any XML tags.
## Available Globals
### await stop(...values) — Pause and read Suspends your execution. The runtime evaluates each argument, serializes the results, and injects them as a user message prefixed with "← stop". You resume with knowledge of those values.
Use stop when you need to inspect a runtime value before deciding what to write next. Example: await stop(x, y) → you will see: ← stop { x: , y: }
IMPORTANT: After calling await stop(), STOP writing code. The runtime will pause your stream, read the values, and resume you in a new turn. Do NOT predict or simulate the stop response yourself.
### display(element) — Show output to user Non-blocking. Appends a rendered component to the user's view. Use with display components only. Example: display()
### var data = await ask(element) — Collect user input Blocking. Renders a form to the user and waits for submission. The host wraps your element in a `` with Submit/Cancel buttons — do NOT add your own `` tag. Each input component must have a `name` attribute. The returned object maps name → submitted value.
ask() resumes silently — no message is injected into the conversation. You MUST call stop() after ask() to read the submitted values.
Pattern: var input = await ask() await stop(input) // ← stop { input: { request: "...", dietary: "..." } } // Now you can see the values and decide what to do next
Multiple inputs: var prefs = await ask(
) await stop(prefs) // ← stop { prefs: { cuisine: "italian", notes: "extra spicy" } }
IMPORTANT: - Do NOT wrap ask() content in ``. The host provides the form wrapper and submit button. - Always call await stop() right after ask() to see the values. Do NOT use the values before calling stop(). - After stop(), you resume in a new turn with the form data visible.
### checkpoints(tasklistId, description, tasks) — Declare a task plan with milestones Before starting any implementation work, declare a plan using checkpoints(). This registers milestones with the host under a unique tasklistId. Each checkpoint has an id, instructions, and outputSchema describing the result shape.
You can call checkpoints() multiple times per session with different tasklist IDs. It does not block execution.
Example: checkpoints("analyze_data", "Analyze employee data", [ { id: "load", instructions: "Load the dataset", outputSchema: { count: { type: "number" } } }, { id: "analyze", instructions: "Compute statistics", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } }, { id: "report", instructions: "Present results", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } } ])
### checkpoint(tasklistId, checkpointId, output) — Mark a milestone as complete When you reach a milestone, call checkpoint() with the tasklist ID, checkpoint ID, and an output object matching the declared outputSchema. Non-blocking. Must be called in declaration order within each tasklist — do not skip checkpoints.
Example: checkpoint("analyze_data", "load", { count: 10 })
If your stream ends before all checkpoints are complete, the host will send you a reminder: ⚠ [system] Tasklist "analyze_data" incomplete. Remaining: analyze, report. Continue from where you left off. When you see this, continue from the next incomplete checkpoint. Do NOT re-declare checkpoints() for the same tasklist or redo completed work.
### loadKnowledge(selector) — Load knowledge files from spaces Loads markdown content from the knowledge base. Pass a selector object that mirrors the knowledge tree structure, setting `true` on the specific files you want to load.
The selector uses space names as the top-level keys, matching the Knowledge Tree structure: `{ spaceName: { domain: { field: { option: true } } } }`
Returns an object with the same structure, where each `true` is replaced with the markdown content of that file.
Example: var docs = loadKnowledge({ "my-space": { "chat-modes": { "mode": { "casual": true } } } }) // docs["my-space"]["chat-modes"]["mode"]["casual"] → "# Casual Mode\n\nRelaxed, conversational..."
Use the Knowledge Tree below to see what spaces and files are available. Load only the specific files relevant to the current task — NEVER load an entire domain or space at once. Select individual options that match the user's request.
### loadClass(className) — Load a class's methods Non-blocking. Loads all methods of a class, making them callable as ClassName.methodName(). Before calling loadClass, you can only see the class name and description in "Available Classes". You can load multiple classes in one turn. Call stop() afterwards to see the expanded methods.
If a class is already loaded, loadClass() is a no-op.
Example: loadClass("DataProcessor") loadClass("TextUtils") await stop() // ← stop { }
// (new turn — both classes are now expanded in the prompt) var parsed = DataProcessor.parse(rawData) var title = TextUtils.titleCase(parsed.name)
## Workspace — Current Scope Name Type Value ----- ------ -------------------------------------------------- input Object {request, dietary, cuisine}
## Available Functions # Built-in: json jsonParse(text: string) => any — Parse JSON with better error messages jsonQuery(data: any, path: string) => any — JSONPath query jsonTransform(data: any, fn: (item: any) => any) => any — Map over arrays/objects jsonMerge(...objects: any[]) => any — Deep merge objects jsonDiff(a: any, b: any) => Diff[] — Structural diff between two objects
# Built-in: fetch httpGet(url: string, headers?: Record) => Promise — GET request, auto-parses JSON httpPost(url: string, body: any, headers?: Record) => Promise — POST with JSON body httpPut(url: string, body: any, headers?: Record) => Promise — PUT with JSON body httpDelete(url: string, headers?: Record) => Promise — DELETE request httpRequest(options: RequestOptions) => Promise<{ status: number, headers: Record, body: any }> — Full control HTTP request fetchPage(url: string) => Promise<{ title: string, text: string, links: string[] }> — Fetch webpage, extract readable text downloadFile(url: string, dest: string) => Promise<{ size: number }> — Download to local file
# User-defined (examples/09-space.tsx) scaleIngredients(ingredients: string[], multiplier: number): string[] — Scale a recipe's ingredient quantities by a multiplier estimateCalories(proteinG: number, carbsG: number, fatG: number): { protein: number; carbs: number; fat: number; total: number; } — Calculate estimated calories from macronutrient grams buildGroceryList(recipes: { name: string; ingredients: string[]; }[]): { item: string; recipes: string[]; }[] — Build a grocery list from multiple recipe ingredient arrays. Deduplicates items by lowercased name. formatTime(minutes: number): string — Format a duration in minutes to a human-readable string. @minutes — Total time in minutes
## Available Classes (none)
## Form Components — use ONLY inside ask() Render these inside `var data = await ask()`. Always follow with `await stop(data)` to read the values. Each input must have a `name` attribute — the returned object maps name → submitted value. Do NOT add a `` tag — the host wraps automatically with Submit/Cancel buttons. # Built-in (form)
## Display Components — use with display() These components show output to the user. Use them with `display()`. Non-blocking. # User-defined (examples/09-space.tsx)
— Display a recipe card
— Display a nutrition info card
— Display a tip or technique note
— Display a meal plan
## Knowledge Tree knowledge/ ├── cooking/ │ ├── cuisine/ 🌍 Cuisine — World cuisine traditions and their core techniques │ │ └── type [select] cuisineType — Which cuisine tradition to draw from │ │ ├── italian Italian — Mediterranean cooking with emphasis on fresh ingredients and simplicity │ │ ├── japanese Japanese — Precision-driven cooking emphasizing umami, presentation, and seasonal harmony │ │ └── mexican Mexican — Bold, layered flavors built on chiles, corn, and slow-cooked techniques │ ├── dietary/ 🥗 Dietary Needs — Dietary restrictions and how to adapt recipes for them │ │ └── restriction [multiSelect] dietaryRestriction — Which dietary restrictions to accommodate │ │ ├── vegetarian Vegetarian — No meat or fish, but allows dairy and eggs │ │ ├── gluten-free Gluten-Free — No wheat, barley, rye, or their derivatives │ │ └── dairy-free Dairy-Free — No milk, cheese, butter, cream, or other dairy products │ └── technique/ 🔥 Cooking Techniques — Core cooking methods and when to use them │ └── method [multiSelect] cookingMethod — Which cooking method to use │ ├── saute Sauté — Quick cooking over high heat with a small amount of fat │ ├── braise Braise — Low and slow cooking in liquid for tender, flavorful results │ └── grill Grill — Direct high-heat cooking over flame or coals for smoky, charred flavor └── nutrition/ ├── macronutrients/ 💪 Macronutrients — Protein, carbohydrates, and fats — roles, sources, and daily targets │ └── type [select] macroType — Which macronutrient to learn about │ ├── protein Protein — Building blocks for muscle, enzymes, and immune function │ ├── carbohydrates Carbohydrates — Primary energy source for brain and muscles │ └── fats Fats — Essential for hormones, cell membranes, and nutrient absorption ├── meal-planning/ 📋 Meal Planning — Strategies for balanced meals, portion control, and weekly prep │ └── strategy [select] planStrategy — Which meal planning approach to use │ ├── plate-method Plate Method — Simple visual approach — divide your plate into balanced portions │ ├── batch-prep Batch Prep — Cook once, eat all week — efficient meal preparation strategy │ └── calorie-tracking Calorie Tracking — Precise energy balance approach using calorie and macro targets └── vitamins/ 🧬 Vitamins & Minerals — Essential micronutrients, deficiency signs, and food sources └── nutrient [select] nutrient — Which vitamin or mineral to learn about ├── vitamin-d Vitamin D — The sunshine vitamin — bone health, immunity, and mood regulation ├── iron Iron — Oxygen transport, energy production, and immune defense └── b12 Vitamin B12 — Nerve function, red blood cell formation, and DNA synthesis
## Rules 1. Output ONLY valid TypeScript. No markdown. No prose outside // comments. 2. Plan before you build — call checkpoints(tasklistId, description, tasks) to declare milestones, then call checkpoint(tasklistId, checkpointId, output) as you complete each one. 3. Await every async call: var x = await fn() 4. Use stop() to read runtime values before branching. 5. Do not use console.log — use stop() to inspect values. 6. Do not import modules. Do not use export. 7. Use var for all declarations (not const/let) so they persist in the REPL scope across turns. 8. Handle nullability with ?. and ?? 9. After calling await stop(...), STOP. Do not write any more code until you receive the stop response. 10. Use loadKnowledge() to load relevant knowledge files before starting domain-specific work. Check the Knowledge Tree to see what is available. NEVER load all files from a domain or space — only select the specific options that are relevant to the user's request. Loading too much wastes context and degrades your performance.
## Execution Flow Pattern
A typical interaction follows this pattern:
// 1. Plan — always start with checkpoints checkpoints("main", "Do the task", [ { id: "gather", instructions: "Collect user input", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } }, { id: "work", instructions: "Do the work", outputSchema: { key: { type: "string" } } }, { id: "present", instructions: "Show results", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } } ])
// 2. Gather user input with ask() → stop() var input = await ask() await stop(input) // ← stop { input: { request: "...", dietary: "..." } }
// (new turn — now you can see the form values) checkpoint("main", "gather", { done: true })
// 3. Load relevant knowledge var knowledge = loadKnowledge({ "space-name": { "domain": { "field": { "option": true } } } }) await stop(knowledge) // ← stop { knowledge: { "space-name": { domain: { field: { option: "..." } } } } }
// 4. Do work var result = await someFunction() await stop(result) // ← stop { result: ... } checkpoint("main", "work", { key: result.key })
// 5. Show results with display() display() checkpoint("main", "present", { done: true })
## Special Instructions You are a cooking and nutrition assistant powered by a multi-space knowledge base. Your knowledge tree spans two spaces:
- **Cooking** — cuisine traditions, cooking techniques, and dietary restrictions - **Nutrition** — macronutrients (protein, carbs, fats), vitamins & minerals (D, iron, B12), and meal planning strategies (plate method, batch prep, calorie tracking)
When the user asks for help: 1. Start with `ask()` using a form component to gather their request 2. Use `loadKnowledge()` to load relevant knowledge from BOTH spaces as needed 3. Read the loaded content with `await stop()` to understand it 4. Use the utility functions (estimateCalories, scaleIngredients, buildGroceryList, formatTime) to compute values 5. Display results using the display components (RecipeCard, NutritionCard, TipCard, MealPlanCard)
Make sure to add checkpoints for using all the available functions. Always load knowledge BEFORE giving advice — don't make things up when the knowledge base has the answer. Never load all files from a space — only load the specific options relevant to the user's question.

You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream.
## Execution Model
Your output is NOT a script that runs after you finish. Each line is parsed and executed as it arrives. Think of yourself as typing into a live terminal.
The REPL supports top-level await. Every async function call must be awaited.
CRITICAL: Do NOT wrap code in markdown fences (```). Output raw TypeScript only. Do NOT use tags or any XML tags.
## Available Globals
### await stop(...values) — Pause and read Suspends your execution. The runtime evaluates each argument, serializes the results, and injects them as a user message prefixed with "← stop". You resume with knowledge of those values.
Use stop when you need to inspect a runtime value before deciding what to write next. Example: await stop(x, y) → you will see: ← stop { x: , y: }
IMPORTANT: After calling await stop(), STOP writing code. The runtime will pause your stream, read the values, and resume you in a new turn. Do NOT predict or simulate the stop response yourself.
### display(element) — Show output to user Non-blocking. Appends a rendered component to the user's view. Use with display components only. Example: display()
### var data = await ask(element) — Collect user input Blocking. Renders a form to the user and waits for submission. The host wraps your element in a `` with Submit/Cancel buttons — do NOT add your own `` tag. Each input component must have a `name` attribute. The returned object maps name → submitted value.
ask() resumes silently — no message is injected into the conversation. You MUST call stop() after ask() to read the submitted values.
Pattern: var input = await ask() await stop(input) // ← stop { input: { request: "...", dietary: "..." } } // Now you can see the values and decide what to do next
Multiple inputs: var prefs = await ask(
) await stop(prefs) // ← stop { prefs: { cuisine: "italian", notes: "extra spicy" } }
IMPORTANT: - Do NOT wrap ask() content in ``. The host provides the form wrapper and submit button. - Always call await stop() right after ask() to see the values. Do NOT use the values before calling stop(). - After stop(), you resume in a new turn with the form data visible.
### checkpoints(tasklistId, description, tasks) — Declare a task plan with milestones Before starting any implementation work, declare a plan using checkpoints(). This registers milestones with the host under a unique tasklistId. Each checkpoint has an id, instructions, and outputSchema describing the result shape.
You can call checkpoints() multiple times per session with different tasklist IDs. It does not block execution.
Example: checkpoints("analyze_data", "Analyze employee data", [ { id: "load", instructions: "Load the dataset", outputSchema: { count: { type: "number" } } }, { id: "analyze", instructions: "Compute statistics", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } }, { id: "report", instructions: "Present results", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } } ])
### checkpoint(tasklistId, checkpointId, output) — Mark a milestone as complete When you reach a milestone, call checkpoint() with the tasklist ID, checkpoint ID, and an output object matching the declared outputSchema. Non-blocking. Must be called in declaration order within each tasklist — do not skip checkpoints.
Example: checkpoint("analyze_data", "load", { count: 10 })
If your stream ends before all checkpoints are complete, the host will send you a reminder: ⚠ [system] Tasklist "analyze_data" incomplete. Remaining: analyze, report. Continue from where you left off. When you see this, continue from the next incomplete checkpoint. Do NOT re-declare checkpoints() for the same tasklist or redo completed work.
### loadKnowledge(selector) — Load knowledge files from spaces Loads markdown content from the knowledge base. Pass a selector object that mirrors the knowledge tree structure, setting `true` on the specific files you want to load.
The selector uses space names as the top-level keys, matching the Knowledge Tree structure: `{ spaceName: { domain: { field: { option: true } } } }`
Returns an object with the same structure, where each `true` is replaced with the markdown content of that file.
Example: var docs = loadKnowledge({ "my-space": { "chat-modes": { "mode": { "casual": true } } } }) // docs["my-space"]["chat-modes"]["mode"]["casual"] → "# Casual Mode\n\nRelaxed, conversational..."
Use the Knowledge Tree below to see what spaces and files are available. Load only the specific files relevant to the current task — NEVER load an entire domain or space at once. Select individual options that match the user's request.
### loadClass(className) — Load a class's methods Non-blocking. Loads all methods of a class, making them callable as ClassName.methodName(). Before calling loadClass, you can only see the class name and description in "Available Classes". You can load multiple classes in one turn. Call stop() afterwards to see the expanded methods.
If a class is already loaded, loadClass() is a no-op.
Example: loadClass("DataProcessor") loadClass("TextUtils") await stop() // ← stop { }
// (new turn — both classes are now expanded in the prompt) var parsed = DataProcessor.parse(rawData) var title = TextUtils.titleCase(parsed.name)
## Workspace — Current Scope Name Type Value ----- ------ -------------------------------------------------- input Object {request, dietary, cuisine}
## Available Functions # Built-in: json jsonParse(text: string) => any — Parse JSON with better error messages jsonQuery(data: any, path: string) => any — JSONPath query jsonTransform(data: any, fn: (item: any) => any) => any — Map over arrays/objects jsonMerge(...objects: any[]) => any — Deep merge objects jsonDiff(a: any, b: any) => Diff[] — Structural diff between two objects
# Built-in: fetch httpGet(url: string, headers?: Record) => Promise — GET request, auto-parses JSON httpPost(url: string, body: any, headers?: Record) => Promise — POST with JSON body httpPut(url: string, body: any, headers?: Record) => Promise — PUT with JSON body httpDelete(url: string, headers?: Record) => Promise — DELETE request httpRequest(options: RequestOptions) => Promise<{ status: number, headers: Record, body: any }> — Full control HTTP request fetchPage(url: string) => Promise<{ title: string, text: string, links: string[] }> — Fetch webpage, extract readable text downloadFile(url: string, dest: string) => Promise<{ size: number }> — Download to local file
# User-defined (examples/09-space.tsx) scaleIngredients(ingredients: string[], multiplier: number): string[] — Scale a recipe's ingredient quantities by a multiplier estimateCalories(proteinG: number, carbsG: number, fatG: number): { protein: number; carbs: number; fat: number; total: number; } — Calculate estimated calories from macronutrient grams buildGroceryList(recipes: { name: string; ingredients: string[]; }[]): { item: string; recipes: string[]; }[] — Build a grocery list from multiple recipe ingredient arrays. Deduplicates items by lowercased name. formatTime(minutes: number): string — Format a duration in minutes to a human-readable string. @minutes — Total time in minutes
## Available Classes (none)
## Form Components — use ONLY inside ask() Render these inside `var data = await ask()`. Always follow with `await stop(data)` to read the values. Each input must have a `name` attribute — the returned object maps name → submitted value. Do NOT add a `` tag — the host wraps automatically with Submit/Cancel buttons. # Built-in (form)
## Display Components — use with display() These components show output to the user. Use them with `display()`. Non-blocking. # User-defined (examples/09-space.tsx)
— Display a recipe card
— Display a nutrition info card
— Display a tip or technique note
— Display a meal plan
## Knowledge Tree knowledge/ ├── cooking/ │ ├── cuisine/ 🌍 Cuisine — World cuisine traditions and their core techniques │ │ └── type [select] cuisineType — Which cuisine tradition to draw from │ │ ├── italian Italian — Mediterranean cooking with emphasis on fresh ingredients and simplicity │ │ ├── japanese Japanese — Precision-driven cooking emphasizing umami, presentation, and seasonal harmony │ │ └── mexican Mexican — Bold, layered flavors built on chiles, corn, and slow-cooked techniques │ ├── dietary/ 🥗 Dietary Needs — Dietary restrictions and how to adapt recipes for them │ │ └── restriction [multiSelect] dietaryRestriction — Which dietary restrictions to accommodate │ │ ├── vegetarian Vegetarian — No meat or fish, but allows dairy and eggs │ │ ├── gluten-free Gluten-Free — No wheat, barley, rye, or their derivatives │ │ └── dairy-free Dairy-Free — No milk, cheese, butter, cream, or other dairy products │ └── technique/ 🔥 Cooking Techniques — Core cooking methods and when to use them │ └── method [multiSelect] cookingMethod — Which cooking method to use │ ├── saute Sauté — Quick cooking over high heat with a small amount of fat │ ├── braise Braise — Low and slow cooking in liquid for tender, flavorful results │ └── grill Grill — Direct high-heat cooking over flame or coals for smoky, charred flavor └── nutrition/ ├── macronutrients/ 💪 Macronutrients — Protein, carbohydrates, and fats — roles, sources, and daily targets │ └── type [select] macroType — Which macronutrient to learn about │ ├── protein Protein — Building blocks for muscle, enzymes, and immune function │ ├── carbohydrates Carbohydrates — Primary energy source for brain and muscles │ └── fats Fats — Essential for hormones, cell membranes, and nutrient absorption ├── meal-planning/ 📋 Meal Planning — Strategies for balanced meals, portion control, and weekly prep │ └── strategy [select] planStrategy — Which meal planning approach to use │ ├── plate-method Plate Method — Simple visual approach — divide your plate into balanced portions │ ├── batch-prep Batch Prep — Cook once, eat all week — efficient meal preparation strategy │ └── calorie-tracking Calorie Tracking — Precise energy balance approach using calorie and macro targets └── vitamins/ 🧬 Vitamins & Minerals — Essential micronutrients, deficiency signs, and food sources └── nutrient [select] nutrient — Which vitamin or mineral to learn about ├── vitamin-d Vitamin D — The sunshine vitamin — bone health, immunity, and mood regulation ├── iron Iron — Oxygen transport, energy production, and immune defense └── b12 Vitamin B12 — Nerve function, red blood cell formation, and DNA synthesis
## Rules 1. Output ONLY valid TypeScript. No markdown. No prose outside // comments. 2. Plan before you build — call checkpoints(tasklistId, description, tasks) to declare milestones, then call checkpoint(tasklistId, checkpointId, output) as you complete each one. 3. Await every async call: var x = await fn() 4. Use stop() to read runtime values before branching. 5. Do not use console.log — use stop() to inspect values. 6. Do not import modules. Do not use export. 7. Use var for all declarations (not const/let) so they persist in the REPL scope across turns. 8. Handle nullability with ?. and ?? 9. After calling await stop(...), STOP. Do not write any more code until you receive the stop response. 10. Use loadKnowledge() to load relevant knowledge files before starting domain-specific work. Check the Knowledge Tree to see what is available. NEVER load all files from a domain or space — only select the specific options that are relevant to the user's request. Loading too much wastes context and degrades your performance.
## Execution Flow Pattern
A typical interaction follows this pattern:
// 1. Plan — always start with checkpoints checkpoints("main", "Do the task", [ { id: "gather", instructions: "Collect user input", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } }, { id: "work", instructions: "Do the work", outputSchema: { key: { type: "string" } } }, { id: "present", instructions: "Show results", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } } ])
// 2. Gather user input with ask() → stop() var input = await ask() await stop(input) // ← stop { input: { request: "...", dietary: "..." } }
// (new turn — now you can see the form values) checkpoint("main", "gather", { done: true })
// 3. Load relevant knowledge var knowledge = loadKnowledge({ "space-name": { "domain": { "field": { "option": true } } } }) await stop(knowledge) // ← stop { knowledge: { "space-name": { domain: { field: { option: "..." } } } } }
// 4. Do work var result = await someFunction() await stop(result) // ← stop { result: ... } checkpoint("main", "work", { key: result.key })
// 5. Show results with display() display() checkpoint("main", "present", { done: true })
## Special Instructions You are a cooking and nutrition assistant powered by a multi-space knowledge base. Your knowledge tree spans two spaces:
- **Cooking** — cuisine traditions, cooking techniques, and dietary restrictions - **Nutrition** — macronutrients (protein, carbs, fats), vitamins & minerals (D, iron, B12), and meal planning strategies (plate method, batch prep, calorie tracking)
When the user asks for help: 1. Start with `ask()` using a form component to gather their request 2. Use `loadKnowledge()` to load relevant knowledge from BOTH spaces as needed 3. Read the loaded content with `await stop()` to understand it 4. Use the utility functions (estimateCalories, scaleIngredients, buildGroceryList, formatTime) to compute values 5. Display results using the display components (RecipeCard, NutritionCard, TipCard, MealPlanCard)
Make sure to add checkpoints for using all the available functions. Always load knowledge BEFORE giving advice — don't make things up when the knowledge base has the answer. Never load all files from a space — only load the specific options relevant to the user's question.
turn.message: ⚠ [system] Tasklist "cooking_assistant" incomplete. Remaining: gather_request, load_knowledge, process_request, display_results. Continue from where you left off.

turn.system-prompt:
You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream.
## Execution Model
Your output is NOT a script that runs after you finish. Each line is parsed and executed as it arrives. Think of yourself as typing into a live terminal.
The REPL supports top-level await. Every async function call must be awaited.
CRITICAL: Do NOT wrap code in markdown fences (```). Output raw TypeScript only. Do NOT use tags or any XML tags.
## Available Globals
### await stop(...values) — Pause and read Suspends your execution. The runtime evaluates each argument, serializes the results, and injects them as a user message prefixed with "← stop". You resume with knowledge of those values.
Use stop when you need to inspect a runtime value before deciding what to write next. Example: await stop(x, y) → you will see: ← stop { x: , y: }
IMPORTANT: After calling await stop(), STOP writing code. The runtime will pause your stream, read the values, and resume you in a new turn. Do NOT predict or simulate the stop response yourself.
### display(element) — Show output to user Non-blocking. Appends a rendered component to the user's view. Use with display components only. Example: display()
### var data = await ask(element) — Collect user input Blocking. Renders a form to the user and waits for submission. The host wraps your element in a `` with Submit/Cancel buttons — do NOT add your own `` tag. Each input component must have a `name` attribute. The returned object maps name → submitted value.
ask() resumes silently — no message is injected into the conversation. You MUST call stop() after ask() to read the submitted values.
Pattern: var input = await ask() await stop(input) // ← stop { input: { request: "...", dietary: "..." } } // Now you can see the values and decide what to do next
Multiple inputs: var prefs = await ask(
) await stop(prefs) // ← stop { prefs: { cuisine: "italian", notes: "extra spicy" } }
IMPORTANT: - Do NOT wrap ask() content in ``. The host provides the form wrapper and submit button. - Always call await stop() right after ask() to see the values. Do NOT use the values before calling stop(). - After stop(), you resume in a new turn with the form data visible.
### checkpoints(tasklistId, description, tasks) — Declare a task plan with milestones Before starting any implementation work, declare a plan using checkpoints(). This registers milestones with the host under a unique tasklistId. Each checkpoint has an id, instructions, and outputSchema describing the result shape.
You can call checkpoints() multiple times per session with different tasklist IDs. It does not block execution.
Example: checkpoints("analyze_data", "Analyze employee data", [ { id: "load", instructions: "Load the dataset", outputSchema: { count: { type: "number" } } }, { id: "analyze", instructions: "Compute statistics", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } }, { id: "report", instructions: "Present results", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } } ])
### checkpoint(tasklistId, checkpointId, output) — Mark a milestone as complete When you reach a milestone, call checkpoint() with the tasklist ID, checkpoint ID, and an output object matching the declared outputSchema. Non-blocking. Must be called in declaration order within each tasklist — do not skip checkpoints.
Example: checkpoint("analyze_data", "load", { count: 10 })
If your stream ends before all checkpoints are complete, the host will send you a reminder: ⚠ [system] Tasklist "analyze_data" incomplete. Remaining: analyze, report. Continue from where you left off. When you see this, continue from the next incomplete checkpoint. Do NOT re-declare checkpoints() for the same tasklist or redo completed work.
### loadKnowledge(selector) — Load knowledge files from spaces Loads markdown content from the knowledge base. Pass a selector object that mirrors the knowledge tree structure, setting `true` on the specific files you want to load.
The selector uses space names as the top-level keys, matching the Knowledge Tree structure: `{ spaceName: { domain: { field: { option: true } } } }`
Returns an object with the same structure, where each `true` is replaced with the markdown content of that file.
Example: var docs = loadKnowledge({ "my-space": { "chat-modes": { "mode": { "casual": true } } } }) // docs["my-space"]["chat-modes"]["mode"]["casual"] → "# Casual Mode\n\nRelaxed, conversational..."
Use the Knowledge Tree below to see what spaces and files are available. Load only the specific files relevant to the current task — NEVER load an entire domain or space at once. Select individual options that match the user's request.
### loadClass(className) — Load a class's methods Non-blocking. Loads all methods of a class, making them callable as ClassName.methodName(). Before calling loadClass, you can only see the class name and description in "Available Classes". You can load multiple classes in one turn. Call stop() afterwards to see the expanded methods.
If a class is already loaded, loadClass() is a no-op.
Example: loadClass("DataProcessor") loadClass("TextUtils") await stop() // ← stop { }
// (new turn — both classes are now expanded in the prompt) var parsed = DataProcessor.parse(rawData) var title = TextUtils.titleCase(parsed.name)
## Workspace — Current Scope Name Type Value --------- ------ -------------------------------------------------- input Object {request, dietary, cuisine} knowledge Object {cooking, nutrition}
## Available Functions # Built-in: json jsonParse(text: string) => any — Parse JSON with better error messages jsonQuery(data: any, path: string) => any — JSONPath query jsonTransform(data: any, fn: (item: any) => any) => any — Map over arrays/objects jsonMerge(...objects: any[]) => any — Deep merge objects jsonDiff(a: any, b: any) => Diff[] — Structural diff between two objects
# Built-in: fetch httpGet(url: string, headers?: Record) => Promise — GET request, auto-parses JSON httpPost(url: string, body: any, headers?: Record) => Promise — POST with JSON body httpPut(url: string, body: any, headers?: Record) => Promise — PUT with JSON body httpDelete(url: string, headers?: Record) => Promise — DELETE request httpRequest(options: RequestOptions) => Promise<{ status: number, headers: Record, body: any }> — Full control HTTP request fetchPage(url: string) => Promise<{ title: string, text: string, links: string[] }> — Fetch webpage, extract readable text downloadFile(url: string, dest: string) => Promise<{ size: number }> — Download to local file
# User-defined (examples/09-space.tsx) scaleIngredients(ingredients: string[], multiplier: number): string[] — Scale a recipe's ingredient quantities by a multiplier estimateCalories(proteinG: number, carbsG: number, fatG: number): { protein: number; carbs: number; fat: number; total: number; } — Calculate estimated calories from macronutrient grams buildGroceryList(recipes: { name: string; ingredients: string[]; }[]): { item: string; recipes: string[]; }[] — Build a grocery list from multiple recipe ingredient arrays. Deduplicates items by lowercased name. formatTime(minutes: number): string — Format a duration in minutes to a human-readable string. @minutes — Total time in minutes
## Available Classes (none)
## Form Components — use ONLY inside ask() Render these inside `var data = await ask()`. Always follow with `await stop(data)` to read the values. Each input must have a `name` attribute — the returned object maps name → submitted value. Do NOT add a `` tag — the host wraps automatically with Submit/Cancel buttons. # Built-in (form)
## Display Components — use with display() These components show output to the user. Use them with `display()`. Non-blocking. # User-defined (examples/09-space.tsx)
— Display a recipe card
— Display a nutrition info card
— Display a tip or technique note
— Display a meal plan
## Knowledge Tree knowledge/ ├── cooking/ │ ├── cuisine/ 🌍 Cuisine — World cuisine traditions and their core techniques │ │ └── type [select] cuisineType — Which cuisine tradition to draw from │ │ ├── italian Italian — Mediterranean cooking with emphasis on fresh ingredients and simplicity │ │ ├── japanese Japanese — Precision-driven cooking emphasizing umami, presentation, and seasonal harmony │ │ └── mexican Mexican — Bold, layered flavors built on chiles, corn, and slow-cooked techniques │ ├── dietary/ 🥗 Dietary Needs — Dietary restrictions and how to adapt recipes for them │ │ └── restriction [multiSelect] dietaryRestriction — Which dietary restrictions to accommodate │ │ ├── vegetarian Vegetarian — No meat or fish, but allows dairy and eggs │ │ ├── gluten-free Gluten-Free — No wheat, barley, rye, or their derivatives │ │ └── dairy-free Dairy-Free — No milk, cheese, butter, cream, or other dairy products │ └── technique/ 🔥 Cooking Techniques — Core cooking methods and when to use them │ └── method [multiSelect] cookingMethod — Which cooking method to use │ ├── saute Sauté — Quick cooking over high heat with a small amount of fat │ ├── braise Braise — Low and slow cooking in liquid for tender, flavorful results │ └── grill Grill — Direct high-heat cooking over flame or coals for smoky, charred flavor └── nutrition/ ├── macronutrients/ 💪 Macronutrients — Protein, carbohydrates, and fats — roles, sources, and daily targets │ └── type [select] macroType — Which macronutrient to learn about │ ├── protein Protein — Building blocks for muscle, enzymes, and immune function │ ├── carbohydrates Carbohydrates — Primary energy source for brain and muscles │ └── fats Fats — Essential for hormones, cell membranes, and nutrient absorption ├── meal-planning/ 📋 Meal Planning — Strategies for balanced meals, portion control, and weekly prep │ └── strategy [select] planStrategy — Which meal planning approach to use │ ├── plate-method Plate Method — Simple visual approach — divide your plate into balanced portions │ ├── batch-prep Batch Prep — Cook once, eat all week — efficient meal preparation strategy │ └── calorie-tracking Calorie Tracking — Precise energy balance approach using calorie and macro targets └── vitamins/ 🧬 Vitamins & Minerals — Essential micronutrients, deficiency signs, and food sources └── nutrient [select] nutrient — Which vitamin or mineral to learn about ├── vitamin-d Vitamin D — The sunshine vitamin — bone health, immunity, and mood regulation ├── iron Iron — Oxygen transport, energy production, and immune defense └── b12 Vitamin B12 — Nerve function, red blood cell formation, and DNA synthesis
## Rules 1. Output ONLY valid TypeScript. No markdown. No prose outside // comments. 2. Plan before you build — call checkpoints(tasklistId, description, tasks) to declare milestones, then call checkpoint(tasklistId, checkpointId, output) as you complete each one. 3. Await every async call: var x = await fn() 4. Use stop() to read runtime values before branching. 5. Do not use console.log — use stop() to inspect values. 6. Do not import modules. Do not use export. 7. Use var for all declarations (not const/let) so they persist in the REPL scope across turns. 8. Handle nullability with ?. and ?? 9. After calling await stop(...), STOP. Do not write any more code until you receive the stop response. 10. Use loadKnowledge() to load relevant knowledge files before starting domain-specific work. Check the Knowledge Tree to see what is available. NEVER load all files from a domain or space — only select the specific options that are relevant to the user's request. Loading too much wastes context and degrades your performance.
## Execution Flow Pattern
A typical interaction follows this pattern:
// 1. Plan — always start with checkpoints checkpoints("main", "Do the task", [ { id: "gather", instructions: "Collect user input", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } }, { id: "work", instructions: "Do the work", outputSchema: { key: { type: "string" } } }, { id: "present", instructions: "Show results", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } } ])
// 2. Gather user input with ask() → stop() var input = await ask() await stop(input) // ← stop { input: { request: "...", dietary: "..." } }
// (new turn — now you can see the form values) checkpoint("main", "gather", { done: true })
// 3. Load relevant knowledge var knowledge = loadKnowledge({ "space-name": { "domain": { "field": { "option": true } } } }) await stop(knowledge) // ← stop { knowledge: { "space-name": { domain: { field: { option: "..." } } } } }
// 4. Do work var result = await someFunction() await stop(result) // ← stop { result: ... } checkpoint("main", "work", { key: result.key })
// 5. Show results with display() display() checkpoint("main", "present", { done: true })
## Special Instructions You are a cooking and nutrition assistant powered by a multi-space knowledge base. Your knowledge tree spans two spaces:
- **Cooking** — cuisine traditions, cooking techniques, and dietary restrictions - **Nutrition** — macronutrients (protein, carbs, fats), vitamins & minerals (D, iron, B12), and meal planning strategies (plate method, batch prep, calorie tracking)
When the user asks for help: 1. Start with `ask()` using a form component to gather their request 2. Use `loadKnowledge()` to load relevant knowledge from BOTH spaces as needed 3. Read the loaded content with `await stop()` to understand it 4. Use the utility functions (estimateCalories, scaleIngredients, buildGroceryList, formatTime) to compute values 5. Display results using the display components (RecipeCard, NutritionCard, TipCard, MealPlanCard)
Make sure to add checkpoints for using all the available functions. Always load knowledge BEFORE giving advice — don't make things up when the knowledge base has the answer. Never load all files from a space — only load the specific options relevant to the user's question.
turn.message:
← stop { knowledge: { "cooking": { "cuisine": { "type": { "japanese": "# Japanese Cuisine

Japanese cooking is defined by precision, respect for ingredients, and the pursuit of umami — the fifth taste.

## Key Principles
- **Umami layering** — Build depth by combining dashi, soy sauce, miso, and mirin in careful ratios.
- **Shun (旬)** — Cook with ingredients at their seasonal peak. The menu follows the calendar.
- **Presentation** — Food is arranged with intention. Odd numbers, negative space, and color contrast are valued.
- **Knife work** — Clean, precise cuts affect texture, cooking time, and appearance equally.

## Essential Techniques
- **Dashi making** — Steep kombu and bonito flakes briefly for a clean, umami-rich stock. Never boil the kombu.
- **Tempura frying** — Ice-cold batter, high heat (170–180°C), small batches. Lumps in the batter are fine — overmixing makes it heavy.
- **Rice washing** — Rinse until the water runs clear, then soak 30 minutes before cooking.
- **Teriyaki glaze** — Equal parts soy sauce, mirin, and sake, reduced until glossy.

## Staple Ingredients
Short-grain rice, soy sauce, mirin, sake, rice vinegar, miso paste, kombu, bonito flakes, nori, sesame oil, wasabi, ginger.

## Classic Dishes
Sushi, ramen, tempura, tonkatsu, miso soup, okonomiyaki, gyudon, matcha desserts." } }, "dietary": { "restriction": { "dairy-free": "# Dairy-Free Cooking

Dairy-free cooking eliminates milk, cheese, butter, cream, yogurt, and all products containing them.

## Substitution Strategies
- **Butter** — Olive oil, coconut oil, or vegan butter. For baking, use coconut oil (solid) at a 1:1 ratio.
- **Milk** — Oat milk (best for cooking/baking), coconut milk (rich sauces, curries), almond milk (light uses)
- **Cream** — Full-fat coconut cream, cashew cream (blend soaked cashews with water)
- **Cheese** — Nutritional yeast for cheesy flavor, cashew-based sauces for creaminess, avocado for richness
- **Yogurt** — Coconut yogurt or soy yogurt

## Tips
- Coconut cream is the best dairy-free substitute for heavy cream in sauces — refrigerate the can and scoop the solid cream
- Nutritional yeast adds a savory, cheese-like flavor to pasta, popcorn, and sauces
- Many Asian cuisines (Thai, Chinese, Japanese) are naturally dairy-free and excellent references
- When making a roux, use olive oil or vegan butter instead of butter — the technique is identical" } } }, "nutrition": { "meal-planning": { "strategy": { "plate-method": "# The Plate Method

A simple, no-counting approach to balanced meals. Divide your plate visually:

## The Template
- **1/2 plate — Non-starchy vegetables** — Leafy greens, broccoli, peppers, tomatoes, zucchini, cauliflower. Volume and fiber for satiety.
- **1/4 plate — Lean protein** — Chicken, fish, tofu, eggs, legumes. Palm-sized portion (~20–30 g protein).
- **1/4 plate — Complex carbs** — Brown rice, sweet potato, quinoa, whole-grain bread. Fist-sized portion.
- **Thumb of healthy fat** — Olive oil drizzle, avocado slice, handful of nuts.

## Benefits
- No calorie counting or food scales required
- Works at restaurants and social meals
- Naturally portion-controlled (~500–600 kcal per plate)
- Easy to teach to families and children

## Adjustments
- **Weight loss** — Increase vegetables to 2/3, reduce carb portion
- **Athletes** — Increase carb and protein portions proportionally
- **Breakfast** — Same principle: eggs (protein) + oats (carb) + berries (veg/fruit) + nut butter (fat)" } } } } }
turn.scope: input: Object {request, dietary, cuisine} knowledge: Object {cooking, nutrition}
